records.ety: [OE. moustre, OF. mostre, moustre, F. montre, LL. monstra. See Muster, v. t.]

defs: Something shown for imitation; a pattern.
An assembling or review of troops, as for parade, verification of numbers, inspection, exercise, or introduction into service.
quotes: The hurried muster of the soldiers of liberty.
See how in warlike muster they appear, In rhombs, and wedges, and half-moons, and wings.

defs: The sum total of an army when assembled for review and inspection; the whole number of effective men in an army.
quotes: And the muster was thirty thousands of men.
Ye publish the musters of your own bands, and proclaim them to amount of thousands.

defs: Any assemblage or display; a gathering.
quotes: Of the temporal grandees of the realm, mentof their wives and daughters, the muster was great and splendid.
Such excuses will not pass muster with God.

records.ety: [OE. mustren, prop., to show, OF. mostrer, mustrer, moustrer, monstrer, F. montrer, fr. L. monstrare to show. See Monster.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Mustered (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Mustering.]

defs: To collect and display; to assemble, as troops for parade, inspection, exercise, or the like.
Hence: To summon together; to enroll in service; to get together.
quotes: All the gay feathers he could muster.
One of those who can muster up sufficient sprightliness to engage in a game of forfeits.

defs: To be gathered together for parade, inspection, exercise, or the like; to come together as parts of a force or body; as, his supporters mustered in force.
